summary: I was born in Colorado Springs, Colorado, but my mother died shortly after my birth. I lived there with my father and siblings for less than three years before being sent away. It was the starting point of my early life before I moved on to other places for education and work.

In 1899, the De La Vergne family sent me to the Hampton Institute in Virginia, where I graduated in 1904. It was a difficult time for me because I was unprepared for life in the South, which had very different attitudes and social norms from what I was used to. Despite the challenges, I completed my education there.

Virginia was where I attended the Hampton Institute from 1899 to 1904. Living there was a challenging experience due to the social environment and cultural differences compared to my upbringing. It was a significant place in my education and personal development.

After graduating, I moved with my father to Oklahoma, then Indian Territory, and settled in Tulsa. Oklahoma was where I launched my first enterprise, publishing the Tulsa Guide in 1906. Later, I moved to Muskogee and eventually left Oklahoma after statehood because conditions changed.

In Tulsa, Oklahoma, I started the Tulsa Guide in June 1906, which was the first Negro weekly in the territory. The paper covered local news and promoted opportunities for Negroes through a column called the Colored Immigration Bureau. Tulsa was my first major business venture after leaving school.

The Tulsa Guide was the first Negro weekly paper in Indian Territory, launched by me in June 1906. It provided local news and information about opportunities for Negroes in Oklahoma, including a special column called the Colored Immigration Bureau. This publication marked my entry into entrepreneurship.

I moved to Muskogee, Oklahoma, because it was the federal headquarters for Indian affairs and had a more favorable climate than Tulsa. There, I went into real estate by establishing the Johnson Investment Company. Muskogee represented a new phase in my business career before I eventually moved north.

In Muskogee, I founded the Johnson Investment Company to pursue real estate opportunities. This was part of my efforts to build enterprises beyond publishing. The company was a key part of my business activities in Oklahoma before I moved on to other ventures.

After Oklahoma's statehood, I moved north to Omaha, Nebraska, where in 1913 I became the first Negro employee at the United States Post Office there. Omaha was also the headquarters for my work with the Lincoln Motion Picture Company, where I managed bookings and publicity. It was an important city in both my postal and film careers.

I worked for the United States Post Office starting in 1913 in Omaha, becoming the first Negro employee there. I balanced this job with my responsibilities as general booking and publicity manager for the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. Later, I continued working at the post office in Los Angeles while involved in film activities.

The Lincoln Motion Picture Company was organized in Los Angeles in 1915 by my brother, Noble Johnson. I joined in 1916 as general booking and publicity manager, working from Omaha to expand the company's reach nationally. The company produced five feature films and was a pioneering enterprise for Negro filmmakers until it disbanded in 1923.

Los Angeles was where the Lincoln Motion Picture Company was founded and where I moved permanently to supervise the making of the film By Right of Birth. I continued working at the post office there while remaining involved with Lincoln until it closed in 1923. The city was central to my film work and later activities.

My brother, Noble Johnson, was an actor with the Universal Film Company and founded the Lincoln Motion Picture Company in 1915. He served as its president but had to resign due to pressures from Universal. Noble's leadership was crucial to the company's early success and mission to produce films by and about Negroes.

The Universal Film Company was where my brother Noble Johnson was under contract as an actor. Pressures from Universal contributed to his resignation as president of the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. This relationship affected the operations and eventual closure of Lincoln.

Kansas City was one of the cities where I arranged showings of Lincoln Motion Picture Company films after starting in Omaha. The screenings there helped expand the company's audience beyond California and Nebraska. It was part of the national booking network I helped establish.

entity: The Realization of a Negro's Ambition
summary: The Realization of a Negro's Ambition was one of the five feature films produced by the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. It was part of our effort to create photoplays by and about Negroes. These films were central to the company's mission during its operation from 1915 to 1923.

The Trooper of Troop K was another feature film produced by the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. It contributed to showcasing Negro stories and talent in early cinema. This film was part of the five productions that defined Lincoln's filmography.

The Law of Nature was one of the five feature films made by the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. These films were important in promoting Negro actors and narratives during the silent film era. The Law of Nature helped establish Lincoln's reputation in the industry.

A Man's Duty was among the five feature films produced by the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. It was part of our pioneering efforts to produce films by and for Negro audiences. These productions were significant in early African American cinema history.

By Right of Birth was a film based on a story I wrote, and it prompted me to move permanently to Los Angeles to supervise its production. This film was the last of the five features made by the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. It marked a turning point in my involvement with the company and film work.

After the Lincoln Motion Picture Company disbanded in 1923, I established the Pacific Coast News Bureau, which operated until 1927. It provided Negro news of national importance at lower rates than other press associations. Many leading Negro journals subscribed to the service, and I often wrote under the name George Perry.

George Perry was the nom de plume I used when releasing news through the Pacific Coast News Bureau. Under this name, I disseminated important Negro news nationally from 1923 to 1927. It was part of my continued commitment to promoting African American contributions and stories.

entity: George P. Johnson Negro Film Collection
summary: I began collecting materials on Negro contributions to films around the time I joined the Lincoln Motion Picture Company. The George P. Johnson Negro Film Collection includes correspondence, clippings, photographs, and documents that preserve the history of Negro actors and film companies. It is a unique archive that identifies many uncredited Negro actors and provides valuable research material.

The George P. Johnson Negro Film Collection is housed at the UCLA Library's Department of Special Collections. This makes the materials I gathered accessible for research and helps preserve the history of Negro contributions to film. UCLA Library plays an important role in maintaining this unique archive.
cues: George P. Johnson Negro Film Collection
